TextENGNew: With the water supply cut off, we could no longer flush the toilet.
The SDF relief unit had set up portable lavatories, but since Dad was strongly opposed to using them, the Maebara family continued to use our flush toilet without running water.
We'd kept the bath water for laundry, so we managed by scooping it into a basin and flushing that way.

According to Dad, the waste of someone carrying the virus was essentially a concentrated mass of it. He'd absolutely forbidden the family from going out to use the communal toilets, saying it was the same as going to get infected.

But there wasn't that much water left in the tub. We couldn't keep flushing without running water indefinitely. And given that the virus had an incubation period of over a month, it wasn't hard to imagine the quarantine lasting even longer than that.
By Mom's conservative estimate, the quarantine would last at least three months.
changeReason: Japanese 軽く見積もって means 'conservatively/lightly estimated (at minimum)'; old uses 'quick estimate' which implies speed/casualness, while new's 'conservative estimate' correctly conveys the 'at minimum' nuance.

TextENGNew: ...Three whole months.
It was the end of June now—which meant the quarantine wouldn't lift until late September, or possibly October.
In other words... the entire summer of 1983 had been carved out from Hinamizawa entirely.
It was a profoundly sad thing...

The first day, we were paralyzed by fear of the virus spreading outside—but once the water trucks and relief supplies arrived from the SDF, and the doctors began their group examinations, we couldn't afford to stay completely shut in any longer.

Some people, Dad among them, still refused to go out. But gradually, one by one, people began venturing outside—whether to secure necessities or simply to be examined by a doctor and put to rest their fear of being infected.

I was one of them. I couldn't stand being cooped up in our shuttered, duct-taped house, watching the television in mute dread with no one but my family. The urge to go outside and confirm that my friends were safe was overwhelming.
Dad dug in and refused to leave the house. Mom, exasperated, went with me to the group examination.
The town council our family fell under had designated the school grounds as the examination site.
Plenty of villagers had gathered there, sorted into lines according to the council's distribution zones.

The contrast between the villagers in their ordinary clothes and the doctors encased head-to-toe in white raincoat-like hazmat suits was deeply unsettling. ...It was impossible not to read it as unmistakable evidence that they suspected all of us of being carriers.
"Ah, Keiichi-kuuun!!"
"Rena!! Thank god—you're safe!!"
"Y-Yeah! I just ran into Mii-chan. She looked fine too!"
"Really?! What about Satoko and Rika-chan?!"
"They're in a different zone, so I think they had to go somewhere else... I've tried calling them a few times, but I can't get through..."
"I've tried calling too, but no luck at all... I guess the lines are just completely overloaded..."

"I heard a rumor that someone who ignored the SDF's directions ran their car into a telephone pole and snapped the lines. ...But someone else said the government had switched the lines to authorized use only, so ordinary people can't get through."
"...Long story short: the information is a mess and everyone's confused..."
Just then, someone slapped me hard on the back. It was Mion.
"Kei-chan!! You're all right—thank goodness!!"
"That's my line!! I'm glad you're safe too!"
Mion made a great show of wiping her eyes, savoring the reunion—even if it had only been a few days.

There's no need to explain this again, but Hinamizawa, by ancient custom, was governed by three old families known as the Three Families. Right now, the Sonozaki family—Mion's family—stood at their head.

According to Mion, the Sonozaki family had taken the lead in directing the unified town council and trying to calm the situation. They were also cooperating with the government and the SDF, which meant they had access to information ordinary villagers didn't.
"Mion, straight answer—what's really going on? Has this virus actually infected everyone in the village?"

"From the briefing this morning, it sounds like that's extremely unlikely. Apparently the infected cases appeared over in Takatsudo—and almost no one lives there. The quarantine went up so quickly that they're saying it probably hasn't reached the Hinamizawa area yet."
"There's some optimism going around that once everyone in Hinamizawa is confirmed clean, the quarantine will start being lifted in stages."
"I-I hope that's true... I feel awful for the people in Takatsudo, but it would be wonderful if Hinamizawa's quarantine ended sooner..."
At that, Mion's eyes sharpened. She grabbed our heads—mine and Rena's—and pulled us close to her own. Then she lowered her voice to a whisper only we could hear.
"...But I don't think we can afford to be that optimistic. I have a radio, you know—I've modified it to pick up police and SDF frequencies. From what I'm intercepting, this isn't nearly that simple."
"After all, Hinamizawa is an isolated mountain village—cut off from everything. They have no idea how a foreign virus could possibly have made its way here."
"...So far, none of the confirmed infected have any history of traveling abroad. Now they're investigating the travel history of every person in the village, looking for another potential carrier."
"So what you're saying is—someone got infected abroad and brought it back here...?"

"Yeah. And apparently the incubation window is wide enough that whoever brought it in might still be incubating it themselves. On top of that, there's no guarantee the person ever traveled abroad at all—they could have come into contact with a carrier somewhere outside the village."
"...My guess is that the quarantine zone won't stay limited to Hinamizawa. Worst case, it could spread to the rest of the prefecture—or further..."
"And there's one more thing...... just between us, but......... there's talk that this one might not be the foreign killer virus they mentioned on TV at all."
changeReason: Old says the virus 'might be different from' the African one (implying two separate viruses); new says it 'might not be' that virus 'at all' (implying misidentification). The Japanese 別物らしい supports the 'entirely different thing' reading.

TextENGNew: "...What do you mean by that?"
"...I'm not entirely sure myself, but some of what I've intercepted suggests that this whole outbreak might be connected to an endemic disease native to Hinamizawa."
"You're saying a killer virus has been sleeping in Hinamizawa all this time?!"

"I'm not sure... The only thing I know is there's an old legend around here about something 'impure' rising from the swamp and attacking the village. It makes you wonder if something has risen from there again."

"But Mii-chan—if that's the case, then we can at least take comfort in something. Because the village wasn't wiped out back then, right? Which means it has to be far less lethal than the virus on TV that annihilated entire villages."

"...According to the legends of old Onigafuchi, anyone possessed by the impurity from the swamp became a demon. In the end, the villagers could do nothing—they were helpless until Oyashiro-sama descended before them."
"...Meaning the village had no escape from that calamity until a miracle arrived."
"That's right. ...Without that miracle, the village would have been destroyed entirely..."

Many of the older villagers seemed to view this whole outbreak as Oyashiro-sama's curse. I could see them scattered throughout the crowd, running prayer beads through their fingers, lifting their voices in prayer, begging for forgiveness.
"If Rika-chan—Oyashiro-sama's reincarnation—were to deny that this was the curse, the panic would probably settle down. My granny is trying to explain that to the SDF people right now."
"...Well, outsiders wouldn't be able to understand what Rika-chama means to this village."
"...Right. ...Setting superstitions aside, I want to see Rika-chan and Satoko safe with my own eyes..."
That's right—Hinamizawa held a unique faith, venerating a guardian deity known as Oyashiro-sama.
The ending that faith depicted—"Oyashiro-sama's curse"—was what a great many villagers were now laying over this outbreak.
changeReason: Old frames it as 'central to that faith was the legend' while JPN describes it as 'the ending that faith depicts' — an eschatological dimension the new captures better.

TextENGNew: And so everyone wanted to see Rika Furude—venerated as Oyashiro-sama's living reincarnation—alive and well, and to hear her declare that this commotion had nothing to do with Oyashiro-sama.
It was the first emergency of this kind the village had ever faced. No one had recovered from the shock, and everyone was searching for something to hold on to.
Yet even if superstition held sway here, this was still a village in modern twentieth-century Japan.
Of course people also wanted to respond calmly, without panic or confusion.
And so many had resolved in their hearts: the moment they saw Rika Furude safe and unharmed, they would regain their composure.
That also meant, of course, that if they couldn't see her—they would remain lost in superstition, wandering in the dark...
